questions.question: Define Service Level Agreement (SLA) in cloud computing and explain its primary purpose.
keyPoints: SLA is a formal contract between cloud provider and customer
Defines measurable service levels (availability, performance, response time)
Specifies remedies for failures (service credits, penalties)
Establishes responsibilities of both provider and customer

questions.question: Explain the concept of 'three nines' (99.9%) availability. How much downtime does it permit per month and per year?
keyPoints: Three nines = 99.9% availability
Monthly downtime: ~43 minutes (0.1% of 43200 minutes)
Annual downtime: ~8.76 hours (0.1% of 8760 hours)
Also called 'high availability' in cloud context

questions.question: What is the shared responsibility model in cloud computing? How do responsibilities differ between IaaS and SaaS?
keyPoints: Model dividing responsibilities between provider and customer
IaaS: Provider manages physical infrastructure; customer manages OS, data, apps
SaaS: Provider manages almost everything except user access and data
PaaS: Shared with provider managing platform, customer managing apps and data

questions.question: Differentiate between SLA and SLO. Why do organizations use both?
keyPoints: SLA is legally binding contract with customer; SLO is internal operational target
SLOs are typically stricter than SLA commitments
SLA provides buffer for provider; SLO drives internal excellence
SLOs help teams aim higher than minimum contractual obligations

questions.question: List and explain any four key components that must be included in a cloud SLA.
keyPoints: Service description and scope
Availability/uptime guarantees (expressed in percentage/nines)
Performance metrics (response time, latency, throughput)
Service credits/penalties for violations
Escalation procedures and support response times
Measurement methodology and reporting intervals

questions.question: What are service credits in SLA context? When and how are they typically calculated?
keyPoints: Service credits are financial rebates to customers for SLA violations
Triggered when provider fails to meet agreed service levels
Credit percentages increase with severity of shortfall
Usually calculated based on monthly measurement period
Example: 10% credit if availability drops below 99.9%, 25% if below 99%

questions.question: Explain the SLA management lifecycle. What are its main phases?
keyPoints: Definition: Identifying service requirements and metrics
Negotiation: Agreeing on terms with provider
Implementation: Technical setup for monitoring
Monitoring: Continuous tracking of agreed metrics
Reporting: Regular performance reports to stakeholders
Compliance: Verifying adherence to SLA terms
Review/Renewal: Evaluating and updating SLA as needed

questions.question: Explain the shared responsibility model in detail for IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS. Provide examples of what the provider manages versus what the customer is responsible for in each model.
keyPoints: IaaS: Provider manages physical hardware, virtualization, network, storage; Customer manages OS, middleware, runtime, data, applications
PaaS: Provider manages hardware, OS, virtualization, development tools, database; Customer manages applications and data
SaaS: Provider manages everything including application, data storage, security; Customer manages user access and some data policies
Must include concrete examples for each (e.g., OS patches in IaaS = customer; in SaaS = provider)
Discuss security responsibilities at each level
Explain how this affects SLA expectations in each model

questions.question: A company is choosing between two cloud providers for mission-critical applications. Provider A offers 99.9% uptime with $10,000 monthly fee. Provider B offers 99.99% uptime with $25,000 monthly fee. Analyze this scenario considering: (a) Downtime differences (b) Cost implications of SLA violations (c) Factors to consider besides uptime.
keyPoints: Calculate downtime: Provider A (3 nines) = 8.76 hrs/year; Provider B (4 nines) = 52 min/year
Cost per hour of downtime for the company's applications
Provider A: Lower cost but higher risk; Provider B: Premium reliability
Consider business criticality: Can company afford 8+ hours downtime?
Other factors: Support quality, geographic redundancy, penalty terms, exit costs, data sovereignty
TCO analysis beyond just monthly fees
If company revenue is high per hour, premium SLA may be justified

questions.question: What are RTO and RPO in cloud disaster recovery? Explain their significance and how they influence cloud service selection and SLA negotiation.
keyPoints: RTO (Recovery Time Objective): Maximum acceptable time to restore service
RPO (Recovery Point Objective): Maximum acceptable data loss in time terms
RTO affects backup frequency, replication strategy, failover mechanisms
Lower RTO/RPO = higher costs (real-time replication, multiple regions)
SLA must specify both for disaster recovery scenarios
Example: RPO of 1 hour means backup at least every hour; RTO of 15 minutes means automated failover required
Discuss how to align RTO/RPO with business requirements and budget
Mention trade-offs in cloud provider selection based on these metrics

questions.question: Discuss the key elements that should be monitored in cloud SLA compliance. How can organizations effectively track SLA metrics and what actions should be taken upon violation?
keyPoints: Elements to monitor: Availability/uptime, latency, throughput, error rates, support response times
Monitoring tools: Third-party monitoring, provider dashboards, synthetic transactions
Measurement methodology: Who measures, what tools, how often reporting
Documentation: Maintain logs for evidence in case of disputes
Upon violation: Document with timestamps, escalate per agreement, claim service credits
Prevention: Regular review meetings, trend analysis, proactive alerting
SLA review meetings: Monthly/quarterly reviews with provider
Importance of clear measurement clauses in contract
